sectionDescription: Master the execution of IFR procedures including holds, departures, non-precision approaches, circling and missed approaches. Covers technique, regulations and decision-making for IPC and airline interviews.

categories.title: Procedures & Approaches
categories.description: Holding patterns, instrument departures, non-precision approaches, circling approaches and missed approach procedures.

modules.title: Holding Patterns – Entry, Timing and Wind Correction
modules.summary: How to determine hold entry, apply timing rules, correct for wind, and handle non-standard holds with confidence.

content.text: Holding patterns are a fundamental IFR skill that examiners and airline interviewers expect you to execute flawlessly. Beyond rote memorisation of entry sectors, you need to understand timing, wind correction and how to adapt when ATC gives you something non-standard.

items: Direct entry: When approaching the fix from the holding side, you fly directly to the fix, turn outbound and begin holding.

Parallel entry: When approaching from the non-holding side at certain angles, you cross the fix, fly parallel to the outbound leg on the non-holding side, then turn back to intercept the inbound track.
Teardrop entry: When approaching from the non-holding side at other angles, you cross the fix, fly a teardrop heading into the holding side, then turn to intercept the inbound track.

content.text: The exact boundaries between entry types are defined by the 70-degree lines from the holding fix. In practice, if you are close to a boundary, any reasonable entry that keeps you in protected airspace is acceptable.

items: Standard holding leg timing is 1 minute inbound at or below 14,000 ft, and 1.5 minutes above 14,000 ft.
Timing starts abeam the fix on the outbound leg or when wings level outbound, whichever is later.
ATC may assign non-standard leg times or DME distances; follow their instructions.
On the outbound leg, apply a triple drift correction to compensate for wind pushing you off the inbound track.
On the inbound leg, apply a single drift correction to track directly to the fix.
Adjust outbound timing to achieve the target inbound leg time, increasing outbound time when you have a headwind inbound.
Left-hand turns instead of standard right-hand turns reverse the protected airspace and entry sectors.
DME holds use distance rather than time for the outbound leg.
Published holds on approach charts may have specific altitudes, speeds or leg lengths that override standard values.

content.question: You are tracking 090 degrees to a hold with an inbound track of 270 degrees and right-hand turns. What entry would you use?
content.answer: This is a direct entry. You are approaching the fix on the holding side (your track is reciprocal to the inbound), so you fly to the fix, turn right to the outbound heading of 090, and begin the hold.

content.question: You are holding at 10,000 ft with a strong tailwind on the outbound leg. Your inbound leg is taking only 40 seconds. How do you correct?
content.answer: Increase your outbound leg time. If the inbound is 20 seconds short, add approximately 20 seconds to the outbound leg to achieve a 1-minute inbound. Continue adjusting until you achieve the target inbound time.

content.question: ATC clears you for a hold with left-hand turns. How does this change your entry determination?
content.answer: The entry sectors are mirrored. What would be a teardrop entry in a right-hand hold becomes a parallel entry in a left-hand hold, and vice versa. Visualise the hold with turns to the left and determine your entry based on which sector you approach from.

content.question: Why is triple drift correction used on the outbound leg?
content.answer: Triple drift compensates for the wind effect during the turn at the outbound end and positions you to intercept the inbound track correctly. Single drift on inbound tracks you to the fix; triple drift on outbound sets up the geometry so that single drift will work on the next inbound.

refs.note: Standard holding pattern procedures and entry methods.

modules.title: Instrument Departures – SIDs, Diverse Departures and Obstacle Clearance
modules.summary: Understanding SIDs, diverse departures, required climb gradients and what to do when you cannot meet published requirements.

content.text: Getting off the ground safely under IFR means understanding how departure procedures protect you from obstacles. Whether you are flying a published SID or a diverse departure, you need to know what climb performance is required and what to do if you cannot achieve it.

items: Standard Instrument Departure (SID): A published procedure with specific tracks, altitudes and often speed requirements designed to provide obstacle clearance and traffic management.
Diverse departure: Where no SID is available or required, a diverse departure allows you to depart in any direction provided you meet the specified climb gradient, typically to a safe altitude.
Obstacle Departure Procedure (ODP): A procedure designed purely for obstacle clearance, separate from traffic management considerations.
The standard diverse departure climb gradient is typically 3.3% (approximately 200 ft per nautical mile) unless otherwise published.
SIDs may require higher gradients, especially in terrain-challenged environments; these are published on the chart.
You must be able to achieve the required gradient at your actual take-off weight under the prevailing conditions, not just book performance.

content.text: What if you cannot meet the gradient?

items: If you cannot meet a SID's climb gradient, you cannot use that SID unless you have an alternative means of obstacle clearance.
Options include reducing weight, waiting for better conditions, or selecting a different departure that you can comply with.
In some cases, a visual climb over the aerodrome to a safe altitude may be an option if conditions permit.
Calculate your actual climb gradient capability during flight planning, not just in the cruise performance tables.
Consider engine-out climb gradients for multi-engine aircraft; some SIDs require all-engine gradients that may not be achievable on one engine.
Brief the departure procedure including altitude constraints, speed requirements and the missed approach point if you need to return immediately.

content.question: A SID requires a 5% climb gradient to 3,000 ft. Your aircraft performance shows 4.5% at your planned weight. Can you use this SID?
content.answer: No. You cannot meet the required climb gradient, so you cannot use that SID for obstacle clearance. You need to reduce weight, find an alternative departure procedure with a lower gradient requirement, or consider other options such as a visual climb if conditions allow.

content.question: What is the typical diverse departure climb gradient and what does it protect you from?
content.answer: The typical diverse departure gradient is 3.3% or about 200 ft per nautical mile. This gradient ensures you climb fast enough to clear obstacles in any direction within the departure area until you reach a safe altitude, assuming no specific departure procedure is required.

content.question: You are departing an unfamiliar aerodrome at night in IMC. There is no published SID. How do you ensure obstacle clearance?
content.answer: You would use the diverse departure criteria, ensuring you can achieve the required climb gradient (typically 3.3%) until reaching the minimum safe altitude for the area. Check the departure area for any higher gradient requirements and verify your aircraft can meet them at your actual weight and in the current conditions.

content.question: An airline interviewer asks why climb gradient matters more at high density altitude. How do you respond?
content.answer: At high density altitude, the aircraft's climb performance is degraded because the air is less dense. The same aircraft that easily achieves 5% at sea level on a cool day might struggle to achieve 3% at a hot, high-altitude aerodrome. This directly affects whether you can safely use departure procedures that require specific climb gradients.

refs.note: Climb gradient requirements and obstacle clearance standards.

modules.title: Non-Precision Approaches – Techniques and Traps
modules.summary: Flying non-precision approaches accurately and safely: step-down fixes, MDA management, the dive-and-drive vs CDFA debate, and common traps.

content.text: Non-precision approaches lack vertical guidance, which means you are responsible for managing your descent profile to reach MDA at a position where you can land if visual. The difference between a good NPA and a dangerous one is in the technique and discipline you apply.

items: No glideslope or vertical guidance; you descend in steps or on a calculated profile.
Minimum Descent Altitude (MDA) instead of Decision Altitude; you level off at MDA until visual or until the missed approach point.
Higher minima and typically less accurate lateral guidance than ILS.
Step-down fixes allow you to descend incrementally as you pass each fix, ensuring obstacle clearance at each segment.
You must positively identify each fix before descending to the next altitude.
Missing a step-down fix and descending early is a serious error that can put you into terrain.
Continuous Descent Final Approach (CDFA): You fly a stabilised descent profile to MDA, treating it like a precision approach. If not visual at MDA, you execute the missed approach.
Dive-and-drive: The traditional method where you descend to MDA, level off, and fly level until reaching the missed approach point or gaining visual reference.
CDFA is now preferred by most operators and regulators because it reduces CFIT risk and produces a stabilised approach profile.
Descending below MDA before having the required visual reference.
Pressing on past the missed approach point hoping to see the runway.
Failing to correctly identify the missed approach point, especially on time-based approaches.
Unstable configuration at MDA because you were too focused on getting down.

content.question: You are flying a VOR approach with two step-down fixes. How do you manage your descent?
content.answer: I descend to the initial approach altitude, then as I pass each step-down fix and positively identify it, I descend to the next published altitude. I continue this until reaching MDA, ensuring I never descend below an altitude until I have passed the fix that allows it.

content.question: What is the advantage of CDFA over the traditional dive-and-drive technique?
content.answer: CDFA keeps you in a stabilised descent with a consistent pitch attitude and power setting, similar to a precision approach. This reduces workload, makes it easier to go around from a stable configuration, and eliminates the dangerous level-flight segment at MDA where pilots are tempted to duck under.

content.question: You reach MDA on a non-precision approach and cannot see the runway. When do you go around?
content.answer: If you are not visual with the required references at MDA, you continue at MDA until the missed approach point. At the missed approach point, if still not visual, you execute the missed approach. With CDFA technique, you would go around at MDA if not visual rather than levelling off.

content.question: An examiner asks what visual references you need at MDA to continue to land. What is your answer?
content.answer: I need to be able to identify the runway environment, which includes elements like the threshold, runway markings, lighting, or other features that allow me to judge my position and fly a safe approach path to landing. A vague glow or partial glimpse is not sufficient.

modules.title: Circling Approaches – Manoeuvring in Limited Visibility
modules.summary: When and how to fly a circling approach, understanding circling minima and protected areas, and handling the transition from instrument to visual flight.

content.text: Circling approaches are some of the most demanding IFR procedures. You transition from instrument flight to visual manoeuvring at low altitude with limited visibility, then must land on a different runway from the approach you flew. Getting this wrong has killed experienced pilots.

items: When the runway aligned with the instrument approach is not suitable for landing due to wind, runway condition or other factors.
When no straight-in approach exists for the landing runway.
When ATC assigns a runway that requires circling from the available approach.
Circling minima are higher than straight-in minima and vary by aircraft category.
The circling protected area is defined by arcs around each runway threshold; you must remain within this area.
Category is based on approach speed (Vat), not aircraft type; a fast light aircraft may need to use a higher category.
Descend to the circling MDA on the instrument approach.
When visual with the runway environment, manoeuvre to position for landing while remaining within the protected area.
Maintain at least circling MDA until in a position to make a normal descent to the landing runway.
Keep the runway in sight throughout the manoeuvre; if you lose visual reference, execute the missed approach.
If you lose visual reference during circling, execute the missed approach immediately.
Turn toward the runway to remain in protected airspace, then follow the published missed approach procedure.
This is a high-workload situation; brief it thoroughly before starting the approach.

content.question: You are circling to runway 27 from an ILS runway 09 approach. What are your priorities during the circling manoeuvre?
content.answer: Maintain at least circling MDA until I can descend normally to runway 27. Keep the runway in sight at all times. Stay within the circling protected area. Be ready to execute the missed approach if I lose visual reference. Manage configuration and speed to allow a stable approach to the new runway.

content.question: What determines which circling category you must use?
content.answer: Circling category is determined by your approach speed (Vat), which is typically 1.3 times the stall speed in landing configuration at maximum landing weight. If your approach speed falls into Category B, you use Category B minima regardless of aircraft size.

content.question: You lose sight of the runway while circling to land. What is your immediate action?
content.answer: Execute the missed approach immediately. Turn toward the runway to remain within protected airspace, then climb and follow the published missed approach procedure. Do not attempt to re-acquire visual reference at low altitude in marginal conditions.

content.question: Why are circling approaches considered high-risk?
content.answer: You are manoeuvring visually at low altitude with limited visibility margins, close to terrain and obstacles, while transitioning between instrument and visual flight. The workload is high, the margins are small, and losing visual reference can happen quickly. Many CFIT accidents have occurred during circling approaches.

modules.title: Missed Approach Procedures – Execution and Decision-Making
modules.summary: When to go around, how to execute the missed approach procedure, and the decision-making that separates safe pilots from accident statistics.

content.text: The missed approach is not a failure; it is the planned outcome when conditions are not met for a safe landing. Airline pilots go around without hesitation because they understand that pressing on is where accidents happen. Your ability to make and execute a go-around decision cleanly is a key indicator of your professionalism.
When to execute a missed approach

items: You reach DA/MDA without the required visual references.
You lose visual reference after going visual but before landing is assured.
The approach becomes unstabilised and cannot be corrected.
ATC instructs you to go around.
Any situation where continuing to land would be unsafe.
Apply go-around power and establish a climb attitude.
Retract flaps and gear as per your aircraft procedure, ensuring positive rate of climb first.
Fly the published missed approach procedure: track, altitude and hold point.
Advise ATC of your missed approach and intentions.
Delaying the decision to go around, hoping conditions will improve.
Not applying full go-around power immediately.
Failing to fly the published missed approach procedure accurately.
Poor communication with ATC about intentions after the missed approach.
Fly to the holding point and assess your options: try again, divert, or hold for conditions to improve.
Review fuel state; a missed approach uses more fuel than a landing.
Consider whether the same approach is likely to succeed on the second attempt or whether conditions have changed.
Make a deliberate decision rather than automatically trying again.

content.question: You are at decision altitude on an ILS and have not acquired visual reference. Walk through your actions.
content.answer: At DA with no visual, I call 'going around' and immediately apply go-around thrust, pitch to climb attitude, and follow the published missed approach. I ensure positive rate before retracting gear, then clean up flaps on schedule. I advise ATC and fly the missed approach procedure to the holding point while planning my next action.

content.question: After a missed approach, you have enough fuel for one more attempt or a divert to your alternate. Weather at destination is unchanged. What factors influence your decision?
content.answer: I consider why the first approach failed and whether a second attempt is likely to succeed. If conditions are marginal and unchanged, a second attempt may produce the same result while burning fuel I need for the alternate. If there was a specific factor like a wind gust or momentary visibility reduction that has now passed, a second attempt might be reasonable. I err on the side of diverting early rather than exhausting options.

content.question: What is the most dangerous mindset on a missed approach?
content.answer: The most dangerous mindset is 'I'll just take a quick look below minima' or 'I'll delay the go-around to see if the runway appears.' This continuation bias has caused many accidents. The missed approach should be an immediate, trained response when the criteria for continuing are not met.

content.question: An examiner asks how you would brief the missed approach before starting an ILS. What key points do you cover?
content.answer: I brief the missed approach altitude, the initial heading or track, any turns required, the hold point, and the expected holding pattern. I also brief the go-around power setting and configuration sequence for my aircraft. The missed approach should be as well-prepared as the approach itself because I may need to execute it with minimal notice.

refs.note: Regulatory requirements for missed approach execution.
